case.note: Case report PMC11761911 / PMID 39857724. Multiparous patient with Hodgkin's lymphoma (nodular sclerosis subtype, diagnosed October 2020) and type 1 diabetes, treated with pembrolizumab during pregnancy. Episode reviewed: pembrolizumab treatment during pregnancy with 18F-FDG PET/CT exposure. Pregnancy dated ~24 weeks on 6 June 2023, estimated LMP 20 December 2022. Successful delivery at 37.5 weeks September 2023.

medications.episode: Treatment given during pregnancy; 11 cycles September 2022 to May 2023; from July 2022 to December 2022 (six cycles of 200 mg) with possible sixth cycle in periconceptional period; 200 mg in February, March, April, May during first and second trimester

ambiguities.question: La fuente menciona 'Brentuximab and Bendamustine, followed by Brentuximab Vedotin'. ¿'Brentuximab' y 'Brentuximab Vedotin' son el mismo fármaco o dos exposiciones distintas? Por favor confirme la identidad para evitar un posible duplicado.

La fuente indica que Brentuximab Vedotin fueron 'four cycles between January 2021 and June 2022', pero también describe esta terapia como posterior a la terapia de segunda línea de 2021. ¿Puede confirmar las fechas de inicio y fin de Brentuximab/Brentuximab Vedotin, ya que el orden cronológico parece inconsistente?

La fuente da varios rangos para pembrolizumab (11 ciclos entre septiembre 2022 y mayo 2023; seis ciclos de julio 2022 a diciembre 2022; 200 mg en febrero, marzo, abril, mayo). ¿Puede confirmar el cronograma correcto de dosis y ciclos de pembrolizumab durante el episodio de embarazo?

intake.sourceSummary: Reporte de caso (PMC11761911) de una paciente con linfoma de Hodgkin y diabetes tipo 1 tratada con pembrolizumab durante el embarazo, con exposición a PET/CT con 18F-FDG. Se extrajo un único caso claramente identificado. Insulina lispro y glargina figuran como tratamiento activo continuo; las quimioterapias previas (ABVD, segunda línea, brentuximab/bendamustina) se clasifican como históricas por finalizar antes del episodio de embarazo con pembrolizumab.

patient.note: Case report PMC11761911 / PMID 39857724. Multiparous patient with Hodgkin's lymphoma (nodular sclerosis subtype, diagnosed October 2020) and type 1 diabetes, treated with pembrolizumab during pregnancy. Episode reviewed: pembrolizumab treatment during pregnancy with 18F-FDG PET/CT exposure. Pregnancy dated ~24 weeks on 6 June 2023, estimated LMP 20 December 2022. Successful delivery at 37.5 weeks September 2023.

report.patient_summary: Paciente multípara con linfoma de Hodgkin (subtipo esclerosis nodular, diagnosticado en octubre de 2020) y diabetes tipo 1, tratada con pembrolizumab durante el embarazo. Recibe tratamiento continuo para la diabetes tipo 1 con insulin lispro e insulin glargine. Entre los tratamientos históricos figura dexametasona como parte de una terapia de segunda línea. La evidencia disponible corresponde a las secciones de interacciones de las fichas técnicas de insulin lispro e insulin glargine.
questions_for_clinician: ¿Se ha planificado, durante los periodos en que coincidan corticosteroides con las insulinas, una monitorización de glucosa acorde con lo indicado en las fichas técnicas?

evidence.claim_text: FDA label drug-interactions section for insulin lispro
evidence.quoted_text: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS The table below includes clinically significant drug interactions with Insulin Lispro. Drugs That May Increase the Risk of Hypoglycemia Drugs: Antidiabetic agents, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor blocking agents, disopyramide, fibrates, fluoxetine, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, pentoxifylline, pramlintide, salicylates, somatostatin analog (e.g., octreotide), and sulfonamide antibiotics. Intervention: Dose adjustment and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Lispro is co-administered with these drugs. Drugs That May Decrease the Blood Glucose Lowering Effect of Insulin Lispro Drugs: Atypical antipsychotics (e.g., olanzapine and clozapine), corticosteroids, danazol, diuretics, estrogens, glucagon, isoniazid, niacin, oral contraceptives, phenothiazines, progestogens (e.g., in oral contraceptives), protease inhibitors, somatropin, sympathomimetic agents (e.g., albuterol, epinephrine, terbutaline), and thyroid hormones. Intervention: Dose adjustment and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Lispro is co-administered with these drugs. Drugs That May Increase or Decrease the Blood Glucose Lowering Effect of Insulin Lispro Drugs: Alcohol, beta-blockers, clonidine, and lithium salts. Pentamidine may cause hypoglycemia, which may sometimes be followed by hyperglycemia. Intervention: Dose adjustment and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Lispro is co-administered with these drugs. Drugs That May Blunt Signs and Symptoms of Hypoglycemia Drugs: Beta-blockers, clonidine, guanethidine and reserpine Intervention: Increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Lispro is co-administered with these drugs. Drugs that may increase the risk of hypoglycemia: antidiabetic agents, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor blocking agents, disopyramide, fibrates, fluoxetine, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, pentoxifylline, pramlintide, salicylates, somatostatin analog (e.g., octreotide), and sulfonamide antibiotics ( 7 ). Drugs that may decrease the blood glucose lowering effect: atypical antipsychotics, corticosteroids, danazol, diuretics, estrogens, glucagon, isoniazid, niacin, oral contraceptives, phenothiazines, progestogens (e.g., in oral contraceptives), protease inhibitors, somatropin, sympathomimetic agents (e.g., albuterol, epinephrine, terbutaline), and thyroid hormones ( 7 ). Drugs that may increase or decrease the blood glucose lowering effect: alcohol, beta-blockers, clonidine, lithium salts, and pentamidine ( 7 ). Drugs that may blunt the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia: beta-blockers, clonidine, guanethidine, and reserpine ( 7 ).

evidence.claim_text: FDA label drug-interactions section for insulin glargine
evidence.quoted_text: 7 DRUG INTERACTIONS Table 8 includes clinically significant drug interactions with Insulin Glargine-yfgn. Table 8: Clinically Significant Drug Interactions with Insulin Glargine-yfgn Drugs that May Increase the Risk of Hypoglycemia Drugs: Antidiabetic agents, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor blocking agents, disopyramide, fibrates, fluoxetine, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, pentoxifylline, pramlintide, salicylates, somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide), sulfonamide antibiotics, GLP-1 receptor agonists, DPP-4 inhibitors, and SGLT-2 inhibitors. Intervention: Dosage reductions and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Glargine-yfgn is coadministered with these drugs. Drugs that May Decrease the Blood Glucose Lowering Effect of Insulin Glargine-yfgn Drugs: Atypical antipsychotics (e.g., olanzapine and clozapine), corticosteroids, danazol, diuretics, estrogens, glucagon, isoniazid, niacin, oral contraceptives, phenothiazines, progestogens (e.g., in oral contraceptives), protease inhibitors, somatropin, sympathomimetic agents (e.g., albuterol, epinephrine, terbutaline), and thyroid hormones. Intervention: Dosage increases and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Glargine-yfgn is coadministered with these drugs. Drugs that May Increase or Decrease the Blood Glucose Lowering Effect of Insulin Glargine-yfgn Drugs: Alcohol, beta-blockers, clonidine, and lithium salts. Pentamidine may cause hypoglycemia, which may sometimes be followed by hyperglycemia. Intervention: Dosage adjustment and increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Glargine-yfgn is coadministered with these drugs. Drugs that May Blunt Signs and Symptoms of Hypoglycemia Drugs: Beta-blockers, clonidine, guanethidine, and reserpine. Intervention: Increased frequency of glucose monitoring may be required when Insulin Glargine-yfgn is coadministered with these drugs. Drugs that Affect Glucose Metabolism : Adjustment of insulin dosage may be needed. ( 7 ) Antiadrenergic Drugs (e.g., beta-blockers, clonidine, guanethidine, and reserpine): Signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia may be reduced or absent. ( 7 ) * Biosimilar means that the biological product is approved based on data demonstrating that it is highly similar to an FDA-approved biological product, known as a reference product, and that there are no clinically meaningful differences between the biosimilar product and the reference product. Biosimilarity of Insulin Glargine-yfgn has been demonstrated for the condition(s) of use (e.g., indication(s), dosing regimen(s)), strength(s), dosage form(s), and route(s) of administration described in its Full Prescribing Information.

unverified_removed.claim_text: ¿La dexametasona sigue estando activa o se ha suspendido, dado que la insulin lispro e insulin glargine continúan como tratamiento para la diabetes tipo 1?
unverified_removed.reason: Revisor narrativo: La pregunta afirma que la insulin lispro e insulin glargine 'continúan como tratamiento para la diabetes tipo 1', lo cual es un hecho del paciente correcto, pero la premisa de que la dexametasona podría 'seguir estando activa' contradice el contexto del paciente donde la dexametasona tiene estado histórico
